subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around you. Whether you live near the busy streets of Mogadishu, the green hills of Hargeisa, the coast of Mombasa, or a village beside the Jubba River, you are surrounded by living things. The acacia tree bending in the wind, the goats nibbling dry grass, the tilapia swimming in a river, the tiny ants marching across the ground, and even the bacteria you cannot see in the soil are all part of one enormous, connected story. In this unit you will learn how all these living things depend on one another and on the non-living world around them.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 6 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain what an ecosystem and a habitat are, and describe the biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) factors within them.
Describe how plants act as producers and capture energy from the sun through photosynthesis.
Classify animals as primary, secondary, and tertiary consumers, and as herbivores, carnivores, and omnivores.
Explain the vital role of decomposers in recycling nutrients back into the soil.
Build and read food chains and food webs, using arrows correctly to show the flow of energy.
Explain why energy decreases at each step of a food chain and why there are fewer top predators than plants.
Describe how living things show adaptations that help them survive in their habitat.
Evaluate how human activities such as deforestation, overfishing, drought, and plastic pollution affect ecosystems.
Suggest conservation actions that protect living things, connecting this to the responsibility of caring for the Earth.

concepts.title: Ecosystems and Habitats
concepts.explanation: An ecosystem is made up of two kinds of things working together: the living things, called biotic factors, and the non-living things, called abiotic factors. Imagine the savanna near Garissa early in the morning. The grass, the acacia trees, the gazelles, the lions, the birds, and the tiny insects are all biotic - they are alive. The sunshine warming the ground, the temperature of the air, the water in a nearby waterhole, the soil beneath your feet, and the wind moving the grass are all abiotic - they are not…
concepts.example: Every habitat must supply four basic needs: food, water, shelter, and space. When one of these is missing or runs short - for example, when a long drought dries up the waterholes - animals must move, adapt, or they may…

concepts.title: Producers: The Foundation of All Life
concepts.explanation: Every food chain begins with a producer. A producer is a living thing that can make its own food. Green plants are the most common producers on land. They do something amazing that no animal can do: they capture the energy of sunlight and use it to make sugar, a kind of food, inside their leaves. This process is called photosynthesis. The word means 'putting together with light'. During photosynthesis a plant takes in sunlight, water from the soil through its roots, and carbon dioxide gas from the air, and it…
concepts.example: Producers are everywhere. On the savanna, the grasses and acacia trees are the main producers. In a farm near the Jubba River, the maize, banana, and mango plants are producers. In the Indian Ocean, tiny floating plants…

concepts.title: Consumers and Feeding Relationships
concepts.explanation: A consumer is a living thing that cannot make its own food, so it must eat other living things to get energy. You are a consumer. So is a gazelle, a lion, a tilapia, a chicken, and a mosquito. Scientists sort consumers into groups in two useful ways: by what they eat, and by where they sit in the food chain. Sorting by what they eat gives us three groups. A herbivore eats only plants - the gazelle, the goat, the cow, and the camel are herbivores. A carnivore eats only other animals - the lion, the hyena, and the…
concepts.example: Sorting by position in the food chain gives us levels called trophic levels. A primary consumer eats producers directly - it is a plant-eater, like the gazelle eating grass. A secondary consumer eats primary consumers -…

concepts.title: Decomposers: Nature's Recyclers
concepts.explanation: When a plant dies, or an animal dies, or an animal leaves droppings, what happens to all that material? It does not simply pile up forever. A special group of living things gets to work: the decomposers. Decomposers are living things that break down dead plants, dead animals, and waste, returning the nutrients locked inside them back into the soil. The main decomposers are bacteria and fungi, but small animals such as earthworms and dung beetles also help. Think of decomposers as nature's recyclers. When a fallen…
concepts.example: Decomposers are easy to forget because much of their work happens hidden in the soil, out of sight. But they are absolutely essential. Imagine if there were no decomposers at all. Dead leaves, dead animals, and waste…

concepts.title: Food Chains, Food Webs, and the Flow of Energy
concepts.explanation: A food chain is a simple way to show how energy passes from one living thing to the next. We draw it with arrows, and here is the most important rule to remember: the arrow always points in the direction the energy flows - that is, from the thing being eaten to the thing that eats it. So we write grass -> gazelle -> lion. The arrow from grass to gazelle means 'energy flows from the grass into the gazelle because the gazelle eats the grass'. A common mistake is to point the arrow the wrong way, toward the food.…
concepts.example: Food webs teach us a powerful lesson: everything is connected. If one part changes, the whole web feels it. Suppose hunters removed all the lions from a savanna. With no lions to hunt them, the gazelle population might…

concepts.title: Human Impact and Caring for the Earth
concepts.explanation: Human beings are part of ecosystems too, and our actions can help or harm them. Sadly, many human activities damage the natural world, and East Africa faces several of these challenges directly. Understanding the problems is the first step to solving them. Deforestation means cutting down forests and trees, often for firewood, charcoal, or to clear land for farming. Trees are producers and homes; when they are removed, the animals that lived among them lose their habitat, the soil is no longer held by roots and…
concepts.example: Plastic pollution is a growing danger, especially on beaches and in the ocean. Plastic bags and bottles dropped on the sand are washed into the sea, where turtles may swallow them thinking they are jellyfish, and fish…

explorations.title: The Race to Rot: Decomposition Investigation
explorations.context: To find out how decomposers break down different materials, and which materials rot and which do not.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you predict. Which will rot fastest: a piece of fruit, a leaf, or a piece of plastic? Complete this sentence: 'I predict that ___ will break down fastest because ___.'
explorations.answer: Which items broke down (decomposed)? Which did not change at all? What living things do you think were breaking down the food and leaf? (Think about the unit.) Why is it important that fruit peels and leaves can rot, but a problem that plastic cannot? How did keeping the soil moist and warm help…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out how decomposers break down different materials, and which materials rot and which do not.

explorations.title: Do Producers Need Light? The Covered Leaf Test
explorations.context: To find out whether a green plant needs sunlight to stay healthy and make its food.
explorations.prompt: Predict what will happen to a leaf if you block its sunlight for a week. Write: 'I predict the covered leaf will ___ because producers need ___.'
explorations.answer: How did the covered leaf change compared with the uncovered one? Why did we leave one leaf uncovered? What is the job of a control in an experiment? What does this show about how producers depend on sunlight? If producers cannot make food, what happens to the consumers that depend on them?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether a green plant needs sunlight to stay healthy and make its food.

explorations.title: A Mini-Ecosystem in a Bottle
explorations.context: To build a small closed ecosystem and observe how living and non-living parts work together over time.
explorations.prompt: Predict whether a sealed bottle with soil, a small plant, and a little water can keep a plant alive without you adding more water. Explain your thinking.
explorations.answer: Where did the water droplets on the inside of the bottle come from? Name the biotic and abiotic factors inside your bottle. How does the water keep being used again and again inside the sealed bottle? How is your bottle like a real ecosystem, such as a forest or a pond?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a small closed ecosystem and observe how living and non-living parts work together over time.

explorations.title: Counting Life: A Backyard Biodiversity Survey
explorations.context: To measure the biodiversity - the variety of living things - in two different areas near your home.
explorations.prompt: Predict which area will have more kinds of living things: a patch of bare, dusty ground, or a shaded area under a tree or bush. Explain why.
explorations.answer: Which area had greater biodiversity - more different kinds of living things? Why do you think that area supported more life? Think about abiotic factors like water and shade. What might happen to the biodiversity if the tree providing shade were cut down? Why is high biodiversity usually a sign of…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To measure the biodiversity - the variety of living things - in two different areas near your home.

methods.title: The Race to Rot: Decomposition Investigation
methods.example: To find out how decomposers break down different materials, and which materials rot and which do not.
steps: Place a little soil in each jar, or dig small shallow holes in the ground.
Bury one item in each jar or hole: fruit peel, leaf, bread, and plastic. Label each one.
Sprinkle a little water on each to keep the soil slightly moist, like after light rain.
Put the jars in a warm, shaded place. Keep the soil slightly damp through the week.
Every two or three days, gently uncover each item, observe it, then cover it again.

methods.title: Do Producers Need Light? The Covered Leaf Test
methods.example: To find out whether a green plant needs sunlight to stay healthy and make its food.
steps: Choose two healthy leaves of similar size on the same plant.
Cover one leaf completely with card or foil so no light reaches it. Leave the other leaf uncovered as your control.
Make sure the plant still gets water and sunlight as normal.
Wait for five to seven days.
Uncover the covered leaf and compare it with the uncovered one.

methods.title: A Mini-Ecosystem in a Bottle
methods.example: To build a small closed ecosystem and observe how living and non-living parts work together over time.
steps: With an adult's help, cut the top off the bottle if needed to place soil and the plant inside, or use a wide-mouth bottle.
Add a layer of soil about three fingers deep.
Plant the small plant or sprinkle grass seeds, and press the soil gently.
Add a small amount of water - just enough to make the soil moist, not flooded.
Seal the bottle with its lid or tape the top back on to close it.

methods.title: Counting Life: A Backyard Biodiversity Survey
methods.example: To measure the biodiversity - the variety of living things - in two different areas near your home.
steps: Choose two areas: one open and dry, one shaded under a tree, bush, or beside water.
Place your square frame on the ground in the first area.
Look carefully inside the square. Count how many different kinds of living things you can find: plants, insects, spiders, worms, birds passing, and so on.
Record each different kind and how many you see.
Repeat in the second area with the same size square.

workedExamples.title: Following the Energy
workedExamples.prompt: Let us work through the energy idea with numbers, step by step. Suppose the grass in one area captures 10,000 units of energy from the sun. Only about ten percent passes to the next level each time.
workedExamples.solution: The grass (producer) holds 10,000 units of energy. The gazelle (primary consumer) eats grass and receives about ten percent of 10,000, which is 1,000 units. The lion (secondary consumer) eats the gazelle and receives about ten percent of 1,000, which is 100 units. If a bigger predator ate the lion, it would receive only about 10 units - very little energy indeed.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a producer? (a) gazelle (b) lion (c) acacia grass (d) earthworm.
workedExamples.solution: (c) acacia grass. Grass is a producer because it makes its own food by photosynthesis; the others must eat.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: An animal that eats only plants is called a: (a) carnivore (b) herbivore (c) omnivore (d) decomposer.
workedExamples.solution: (b) herbivore. A herbivore eats only plants, like a goat or gazelle.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Which organism is a decomposer? (a) grass (b) fungus (c) gazelle (d) heron.
workedExamples.solution: (b) fungus. Fungi break down dead matter and return nutrients to the soil, so they are decomposers.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: About how much energy passes from one level of a food chain to the next? (a) 100 percent (b) 50 percent (c) 10 percent (d) 0 percent.
workedExamples.solution: (c) 10 percent. About ninety percent is lost as heat and through living and moving, so only about ten percent passes on.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: In the chain plankton to tilapia to Nile perch, the tilapia is the: (a) producer (b) primary consumer (c) secondary consumer (d) decomposer.
workedExamples.solution: (b) primary consumer. The tilapia eats the producer (plankton) directly, so it is a primary consumer.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: A non-living part of an ecosystem, such as sunlight or water, is called a: (a) biotic factor (b) abiotic factor (c) consumer (d) producer.
workedExamples.solution: (b) abiotic factor. Abiotic means non-living, such as sunlight, water, soil, and temperature.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Why are there usually fewer lions than gazelles in a savanna? (a) lions are lazy (b) energy decreases up the chain (c) gazelles eat lions (d) lions do not need food.
workedExamples.solution: (b) energy decreases up the chain. With little energy left at the top, only a few lions can be supported.

practice.prompt: In a food chain, the arrow points: (a) from the eater to the food (b) from the food to the eater (c) in both directions (d) it does not matter.
practice.answer: (b) from the food to the eater. The arrow shows the direction energy flows, into the animal that eats.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a producer? (a) gazelle (b) lion (c) acacia grass (d) earthworm.
practice.answer: (c) acacia grass. Grass is a producer because it makes its own food by photosynthesis; the others must eat.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An animal that eats only plants is called a: (a) carnivore (b) herbivore (c) omnivore (d) decomposer.
practice.answer: (b) herbivore. A herbivore eats only plants, like a goat or gazelle.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which organism is a decomposer? (a) grass (b) fungus (c) gazelle (d) heron.
practice.answer: (b) fungus. Fungi break down dead matter and return nutrients to the soil, so they are decomposers.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: About how much energy passes from one level of a food chain to the next? (a) 100 percent (b) 50 percent (c) 10 percent (d) 0 percent.
practice.answer: (c) 10 percent. About ninety percent is lost as heat and through living and moving, so only about ten percent passes on.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In the chain plankton to tilapia to Nile perch, the tilapia is the: (a) producer (b) primary consumer (c) secondary consumer (d) decomposer.
practice.answer: (b) primary consumer. The tilapia eats the producer (plankton) directly, so it is a primary consumer.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A non-living part of an ecosystem, such as sunlight or water, is called a: (a) biotic factor (b) abiotic factor (c) consumer (d) producer.
practice.answer: (b) abiotic factor. Abiotic means non-living, such as sunlight, water, soil, and temperature.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why are there usually fewer lions than gazelles in a savanna? (a) lions are lazy (b) energy decreases up the chain (c) gazelles eat lions (d) lions do not need food.
practice.answer: (b) energy decreases up the chain. With little energy left at the top, only a few lions can be supported.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which human activity harms ocean food webs by taking too many fish? (a) conservation (b) photosynthesis (c) overfishing (d) composting.
practice.answer: (c) overfishing. Catching fish faster than they can breed collapses fish populations and the ocean food web.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The camel's hump, storing fat for the dry season, is an example of a: (a) food chain (b) decomposer (c) adaptation (d) producer.
practice.answer: (c) adaptation. Storing fat in the hump is a feature that helps the camel survive dry seasons.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What would most likely happen if all decomposers disappeared? (a) nutrients would return to the soil faster (b) dead matter would pile up and soil would grow poor (c) plants would grow better (d) nothing would change.
practice.answer: (b) dead matter would pile up and the soil would grow poor. Nutrients would stay locked in dead things, so producers could not grow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is the correct order of a food chain? (a) lion to gazelle to grass (b) grass to gazelle to lion (c) gazelle to grass to lion (d) lion to grass to gazelle.
practice.answer: (b) grass to gazelle to lion. Energy flows from the producer, to the plant-eater, to the meat-eater.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Race to Rot: Decomposition Investigation
activities.materials: Three or four small clear jars or cups, or three shallow holes in garden soil; A small piece of fruit peel (banana or mango); A green leaf; A small piece of bread; A small piece of plastic (bottle cap or bag); Some soil, a little water, and a notebook
steps: Place a little soil in each jar, or dig small shallow holes in the ground.
Bury one item in each jar or hole: fruit peel, leaf, bread, and plastic. Label each one.
Sprinkle a little water on each to keep the soil slightly moist, like after light rain.
Put the jars in a warm, shaded place. Keep the soil slightly damp through the week.
Every two or three days, gently uncover each item, observe it, then cover it again.

activities.title: Do Producers Need Light? The Covered Leaf Test
activities.materials: A healthy potted plant, or a plant growing in the ground (a herb, bean, or any leafy plant); A piece of card, foil, or dark paper; A paper clip, tape, or string to hold it; A notebook
steps: Choose two healthy leaves of similar size on the same plant.
Cover one leaf completely with card or foil so no light reaches it. Leave the other leaf uncovered as your control.
Make sure the plant still gets water and sunlight as normal.
Wait for five to seven days.
Uncover the covered leaf and compare it with the uncovered one.

activities.title: A Mini-Ecosystem in a Bottle
activities.materials: A large clear plastic bottle with a lid (a used water or soda bottle, cleaned); Some soil; A tiny plant or a few grass seeds, or a small seedling; A little water; A sunny windowsill and a notebook
steps: With an adult's help, cut the top off the bottle if needed to place soil and the plant inside, or use a wide-mouth bottle.
Add a layer of soil about three fingers deep.
Plant the small plant or sprinkle grass seeds, and press the soil gently.
Add a small amount of water - just enough to make the soil moist, not flooded.
Seal the bottle with its lid or tape the top back on to close it.

activities.title: Counting Life: A Backyard Biodiversity Survey
activities.materials: A notebook and pencil; A simple frame such as a bent wire or a square drawn with string (about one big step on each side); Optional: a magnifying glass
steps: Choose two areas: one open and dry, one shaded under a tree, bush, or beside water.
Place your square frame on the ground in the first area.
Look carefully inside the square. Count how many different kinds of living things you can find: plants, insects, spiders, worms, birds passing, and so on.
Record each different kind and how many you see.
Repeat in the second area with the same size square.

activities.title: Observe: Food Chains, Food Webs, and the Flow of Energy
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of food chains, food webs, and the flow of energy around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Human Impact and Caring for the Earth
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of human impact and caring for the earth around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Ecosystem
All living things in an area plus the non-living things they interact with
Habitat
The natural home where an organism finds food, water, shelter, and space
Biotic factor
A living part of an ecosystem (plants, animals, bacteria, fungi)
Abiotic factor
A non-living part of an ecosystem (sunlight, water, soil, temperature, wind)
Producer
A living thing that makes its own food using sunlight (grass, trees, plankton)
Photosynthesis
The process by which producers use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to make food
Consumer
A living thing that eats other living things for energy
Herbivore
A consumer that eats only plants (gazelle, goat, camel)
Carnivore
A consumer that eats only animals (lion, hyena)
Omnivore
A consumer that eats both plants and animals (human, chicken)
Primary consumer
Eats producers directly; the second trophic level
Secondary consumer
Eats primary consumers; the third trophic level

vocabulary.meaning: All living things in an area plus the non-living things they interact with
vocabulary.example: Explain what an ecosystem and a habitat are, and describe the biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) factors within them.

vocabulary.meaning: The natural home where an organism finds food, water, shelter, and space
vocabulary.example: Explain what an ecosystem and a habitat are, and describe the biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) factors within them.

vocabulary.meaning: A living part of an ecosystem (plants, animals, bacteria, fungi)
A non-living part of an ecosystem (sunlight, water, soil, temperature, wind)
A living thing that makes its own food using sunlight (grass, trees, plankton)
vocabulary.example: Every food chain begins with a producer.

vocabulary.meaning: The process by which producers use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to make food
vocabulary.example: Describe how plants act as producers and capture energy from the sun through photosynthesis.

vocabulary.meaning: A living thing that eats other living things for energy
vocabulary.example: A consumer is a living thing that cannot make its own food, so it must eat other living things to get energy.

vocabulary.meaning: A consumer that eats only plants (gazelle, goat, camel)
vocabulary.example: A herbivore eats only plants - the gazelle, the goat, the cow, and the camel are herbivores.

vocabulary.meaning: A consumer that eats only animals (lion, hyena)
vocabulary.example: A carnivore eats only other animals - the lion, the hyena, and the fish-eating heron are carnivores.

vocabulary.meaning: A consumer that eats both plants and animals (human, chicken)
vocabulary.example: An omnivore eats both plants and animals - human beings are omnivores, and so are chickens and baboons.

vocabulary.meaning: Eats producers directly; the second trophic level
vocabulary.example: A primary consumer eats producers directly - it is a plant-eater, like the gazelle eating grass.

vocabulary.meaning: Eats primary consumers; the third trophic level
vocabulary.example: A secondary consumer eats primary consumers - like a snake eating a mouse, or a small fish eating tiny water animals.

commonMistakes: Drawing the food-chain arrow toward the food
The arrow shows energy flow, which goes into the eater, not the food
They recycle nutrients that producers need to grow
Believing energy stays the same up a chain
About ninety percent is lost at every step
Calling every animal a consumer of the same level
Animals sit at different trophic levels depending on what they eat
Thinking plastic will rot like a leaf
Plastic is not natural; decomposers cannot break it down for centuries

connections.text: Just as your digestive system gets energy from food, animals in a food chain pass energy along by eating.
Decomposition is a kind of change where materials break down - a bridge to studying how substances change.
Ecosystems link to the savanna, coast, and Rift Valley you study in geography, and to the dry and wet seasons.
Being a khalifah, a caretaker of the Earth, connects science to the value of protecting creation.
The ten percent energy rule uses division and percentages you practise in maths.
Observe a food chain near your home for a week and draw it accurately.

fluency.prompt: In a food chain, the arrow points: (a) from the eater to the food (b) from the food to the eater (c) in both directions (d) it does not matter.
fluency.answer: (b) from the food to the eater. The arrow shows the direction energy flows, into the animal that eats.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) herbivore. A herbivore eats only plants, like a goat or gazelle.

fluency.prompt: Which organism is a decomposer? (a) grass (b) fungus (c) gazelle (d) heron.
fluency.answer: (b) fungus. Fungi break down dead matter and return nutrients to the soil, so they are decomposers.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.prompt: About how much energy passes from one level of a food chain to the next? (a) 100 percent (b) 50 percent (c) 10 percent (d) 0 percent.
fluency.answer: (c) 10 percent. About ninety percent is lost as heat and through living and moving, so only about ten percent passes on.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.prompt: In the chain plankton to tilapia to Nile perch, the tilapia is the: (a) producer (b) primary consumer (c) secondary consumer (d) decomposer.
fluency.answer: (b) primary consumer. The tilapia eats the producer (plankton) directly, so it is a primary consumer.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) primary consumer. The tilapia eats the producer (plankton) directly, so it is a primary consumer.

fluency.prompt: A non-living part of an ecosystem, such as sunlight or water, is called a: (a) biotic factor (b) abiotic factor (c) consumer (d) producer.
fluency.answer: (b) abiotic factor. Abiotic means non-living, such as sunlight, water, soil, and temperature.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) abiotic factor. Abiotic means non-living, such as sunlight, water, soil, and temperature.

fluency.prompt: Why are there usually fewer lions than gazelles in a savanna? (a) lions are lazy (b) energy decreases up the chain (c) gazelles eat lions (d) lions do not need food.
fluency.answer: (b) energy decreases up the chain. With little energy left at the top, only a few lions can be supported.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) energy decreases up the chain. With little energy left at the top, only a few lions can be supported.

fluency.prompt: Which human activity harms ocean food webs by taking too many fish? (a) conservation (b) photosynthesis (c) overfishing (d) composting.
fluency.answer: (c) overfishing. Catching fish faster than they can breed collapses fish populations and the ocean food web.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) overfishing. Catching fish faster than they can breed collapses fish populations and the ocean food web.

fluency.prompt: The camel's hump, storing fat for the dry season, is an example of a: (a) food chain (b) decomposer (c) adaptation (d) producer.
fluency.answer: (c) adaptation. Storing fat in the hump is a feature that helps the camel survive dry seasons.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) adaptation. Storing fat in the hump is a feature that helps the camel survive dry seasons.

fluency.prompt: What would most likely happen if all decomposers disappeared? (a) nutrients would return to the soil faster (b) dead matter would pile up and soil would grow poor (c) plants would grow better (d) nothing would change.
fluency.answer: (b) dead matter would pile up and the soil would grow poor. Nutrients would stay locked in dead things, so producers could not grow.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) dead matter would pile up and the soil would grow poor. Nutrients would stay locked in dead things, so producers could not grow.

fluency.prompt: Which is the correct order of a food chain? (a) lion to gazelle to grass (b) grass to gazelle to lion (c) gazelle to grass to lion (d) lion to grass to gazelle.
fluency.answer: (b) grass to gazelle to lion. Energy flows from the producer, to the plant-eater, to the meat-eater.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) grass to gazelle to lion. Energy flows from the producer, to the plant-eater, to the meat-eater.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to solve these real-life problems.
realProblems.answer: Gazelle receives ten percent of 8,000 = 800 units. Lion receives ten percent of 800 = 80 units. Working: 8,000 divided by 10 = 800; 800 divided by 10 = 80.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Gazelle receives ten percent of 8,000 = 800 units. Lion receives ten percent of 800 = 80 units. Working: 8,000 divided by 10 = 800; 800 divided by 10 = 80.

realProblems.prompt: A grass patch captures 8,000 units of energy from the sun. Using the ten percent rule, how much energy reaches the gazelle that eats the grass, and how much reaches the lion that eats the gazelle? Show your working.
realProblems.answer: This is likely overfishing - fish are being caught faster than they can breed and replace themselves, so populations shrink. Actions: agree on catch limits and closed seasons, use larger net holes so young fish escape, and protect breeding areas like mangroves. (Any two sensible actions.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: This is likely overfishing - fish are being caught faster than they can breed and replace themselves, so populations shrink. Actions: agree on catch limits and closed seasons, use larger net holes so young fish escape, and protect breeding areas like mangroves. (Any two sensible actions.)

realProblems.prompt: Fishermen near the coast are catching fewer and smaller fish each year. Explain what may be happening and suggest two actions the community could take.
realProblems.answer: Cutting many trees causes: loss of habitat for animals that lived in or around the trees; soil erosion because roots no longer hold the soil; fewer producers and possibly less rainfall. (Any two.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Cutting many trees causes: loss of habitat for animals that lived in or around the trees; soil erosion because roots no longer hold the soil; fewer producers and possibly less rainfall. (Any two.)

realProblems.prompt: A family cuts many trees near their home for charcoal. Describe two effects this could have on the local ecosystem.
realProblems.answer: Amina could: organise a beach clean-up, carry reusable bags and bottles, set up bins, refuse single-use plastic, and teach others why plastic harms sea life. (Any three practical actions.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Amina could: organise a beach clean-up, carry reusable bags and bottles, set up bins, refuse single-use plastic, and teach others why plastic harms sea life. (Any three practical actions.)

realProblems.prompt: Amina wants to reduce plastic pollution on her local beach. Suggest three practical actions she and her friends could take.
realProblems.answer: The waterhole drying up means less water (an abiotic factor). Grass dries and dies, so gazelles have less food and may leave or die; with fewer gazelles, the lions have less prey and must move or go hungry. The whole chain is affected because each level depends on the one below.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The waterhole drying up means less water (an abiotic factor). Grass dries and dies, so gazelles have less food and may leave or die; with fewer gazelles, the lions have less prey and must move or go hungry. The whole chain is affected because each level depends on the one below.

realProblems.prompt: During a long drought, a waterhole dries up. Explain the chain of effects on the grass, the gazelles, and the lions.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a producer and a consumer?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A producer makes its own food using sunlight (for example, grass), while a consumer cannot and must eat other living things (for example, a gazelle). Producers begin every food chain.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why the arrow in a food chain always points toward the animal that eats.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The arrow shows the direction that energy flows. Energy moves from the food into the animal that eats it, so the arrow points toward the eater. Grass -> gazelle, not the reverse.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name two decomposers and describe the job they do in an ecosystem.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Two decomposers are bacteria and fungi (earthworms and dung beetles are also acceptable). They break down dead plants, dead animals, and waste, returning nutrients to the soil for producers to reuse.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why is only about ten percent of energy passed on at each step of a food chain?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: At each step, most energy is used up by the animal for living, moving, and breathing, and is lost as heat. Only about ten percent is stored in the body and available to the next animal.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one adaptation of the camel and explain how it helps in dry, semi-arid land.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The camel stores fat in its hump for energy when food is scarce (other valid answers: long eyelashes against sand, wide feet, going days without water). This helps it survive long, dry periods.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a food chain and a food web?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A food chain shows one line of feeding relationships (grass -> gazelle -> lion). A food web joins many food chains together to show all the feeding links in an ecosystem, giving a truer picture.

questions.question: In a food chain, the arrow points:
options: from the eater to the food
from the food to the eater
questions.answer: from the food to the eater
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: from the food to the eater. The arrow shows the direction energy flows, into the animal that eats.

questions.question: Which of these is a producer?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: acacia grass. Grass is a producer because it makes its own food by photosynthesis; the others must eat.

questions.question: An animal that eats only plants is called a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: herbivore. A herbivore eats only plants, like a goat or gazelle.

questions.question: Which organism is a decomposer?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: fungus. Fungi break down dead matter and return nutrients to the soil, so they are decomposers.

questions.question: About how much energy passes from one level of a food chain to the next?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 10 percent. About ninety percent is lost as heat and through living and moving, so only about ten percent passes on.

questions.question: In the chain plankton to tilapia to Nile perch, the tilapia is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: primary consumer. The tilapia eats the producer (plankton) directly, so it is a primary consumer.

questions.question: A non-living part of an ecosystem, such as sunlight or water, is called a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: abiotic factor. Abiotic means non-living, such as sunlight, water, soil, and temperature.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can explain what an ecosystem and a habitat are, and describe the biotic (living) and abiotic (non-living) factors within them.
I can describe how plants act as producers and capture energy from the sun through photosynthesis.
I can classify animals as primary, secondary, and tertiary consumers, and as herbivores, carnivores, and omnivores.
I can explain the vital role of decomposers in recycling nutrients back into the soil.
I can build and read food chains and food webs, using arrows correctly to show the flow of energy.
I can explain why energy decreases at each step of a food chain and why there are fewer top predators than plants.
I can describe how living things show adaptations that help them survive in their habitat.
